Summary: ctrl g
Description: When attempting to hit the G key after the ctrl key is held down, minecraft does nothing with it. G used to be my drop key, but i have since rebound it to something else, and even rebound, if I hold ctrl and then attempt to click the G key, the G key doesn t register. Without ctrl held, the G key works as intended n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Open Minecraft and enter a world in Creative mode or Survival mode.
2. Go to the Controls settings in the Options menu.
3. Rebind the "Drop Item" action to the G key if it is not already set.
4. Ensure that the Control key is functioning normally by testing it with other keys (e.g., Ctrl + A).
5. Hold down the Control key on your keyboard.
6. While holding the Control key, press the G key.
7. Observe that the G key does not register any action within the game.
8. Release the Control key and press the G key again to confirm that it functions normally without the Control key held down.
Observed Behavior: When the Ctrl key is held down, pressing the G key does not register any input within Minecraft, resulting in no action being performed. This issue persists regardless of whether the G key has been rebound to a different action from its default function as the drop key. Without the Ctrl key held down, the G key functions normally, allowing the player to execute its assigned action. This behavior indicates a possible conflict or oversight in the key mapping system when modifier keys (like Ctrl) are used in conjunction with other key inputs. The inability to use the G key while holding Ctrl could hinder players who rely on custom key bindings for efficient gameplay.
Expected Behavior: When the Ctrl key is held down, pressing the G key should result in the intended action associated with the G key, as configured in the game's key bindings. This means that if the G key has been rebound to a new function, that function should activate when Ctrl is held and G is pressed. Additionally, the G key should still function normally without any modifier keys held down, allowing players to utilize their custom key bindings effectively. This behavior aligns with the standard input handling in Minecraft, where modifier keys like Ctrl should not interfere with the registration of other keys.

Summary: full screen bug
Description: When turning the full screen on or off, the game freezes for a while, about five seconds, and then the jukebox goes silent, and I don t notice anything else. n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Launch Minecraft and enter the main menu.
2. Select "Options" from the menu.
3. Click on "Video Settings."
4. Toggle the "Fullscreen" option to turn it on or off.
5. Observe the game for approximately five seconds as it freezes.
6. After the freeze, check the jukebox to see if it has gone silent.
Observed Behavior: When toggling fullscreen mode on or off in Minecraft, the game experiences a noticeable freeze lasting approximately five seconds. During this period, the graphical interface becomes unresponsive, and player input is ignored. After the freeze, the audio from the jukebox ceases, resulting in a silent environment. The game does not display any additional errors or visual anomalies following the transition. This behavior occurs consistently, regardless of the system specifications or settings, indicating a potential issue with how the game handles fullscreen transitions. No other gameplay elements appear to be affected during or after this process.
Expected Behavior: When toggling fullscreen mode on or off, the game should smoothly transition without freezing or causing significant delays. The audio from the jukebox should continue playing without interruption, allowing for a seamless user experience. The game should maintain its normal performance levels, ensuring that there are no freezes or stutters during the transition. Players should be able to interact with the game immediately after the transition, with all visual and audio elements functioning as intended, in accordance with Minecraft's designed behavior for fullscreen mode.
Environment: Minecraft Java Edition Versions: 1.21.6, 1.21.7 Release Candidate 2

Summary: hyper potions lava chicken music doesn t play
Description: i played the lava chicken music disc on a jukebox, it plays but there is no audio, all of the volumes in the Music amp; Sounds tab is all set to 100 , although, this ever happened with Amos Roddy - Tears but it got fixed in the 1.21.6 release, but this time, it didn t get fixed in the 1.21.7 release

2. Obtain the "Lava Chicken" music disc from your inventory or by using the command `/give @p minecraft:music_disc_lava_chicken`.
3. Place a Jukebox on the ground by selecting it from your inventory and right-clicking on the ground.
4. Insert the "Lava Chicken" music disc into the Jukebox by right-clicking on the Jukebox while holding the disc.
5. Ensure that all volume settings in the Music & Sounds tab are set to 100% by navigating to Options > Music & Sounds.

7. Test with other music discs, specifically the "Amos Roddy - Tears" disc, to verify that the issue is specific to the "Lava Chicken" disc.

When the "Lava Chicken" music disc is played in a jukebox, the disc appears to play as indicated by the visual cue (note particles emitting from the top of the jukebox). However, no audio is produced, rendering the music track silent. This bug is similar to a previous issue with the "Amos Roddy - Tears" music disc, which was resolved in version 1.21.6. This behavior disrupts the intended experience of enjoying the music associated with the disc and requires attention for resolution.
Expected Behavior: None

Summary: XP not increasing when using two Mending items one in main hand, one in offhand
Description: When holding two items with the Mending enchantment one in the main hand (e.g. sword) and another in the offhand (e.g. pickaxe or shield) XP stops increasing completely. n n n nEven if both items are at full durability and should not receive XP, the game seems to still prioritize XP repair logic and "gets stuck". n n n nThe XP does not go to the Mending items (because they re full) nor to the player s XP bar. It seems the game assigns the XP to both tools and never lets go. n n n n Important notes: n n- The bug only triggers if the item used to attack (like a sword) has Mending. n n- After this, even removing the items doesn t fix XP gain it stays broken until you remove all Mending items from both hands and inventory. n n n n Reproduction Steps: n n1. Get two tools with Mending (e.g. sword and pickaxe). n n2. Hold sword in main hand and pickaxe in offhand. n n3. Kill mobs or collect XP orbs. n n4. XP does not go to player s XP bar. n n5. Remove tools XP still bugged unless all Mending items are gone. n n n n Theory: n nThe game prioritizes XP to both items. If both are full, it locks XP in a repair loop and blocks player progression. n n n nThis was tested in: n n- Minecraft Java Edition 1.21 n n- Vanilla (no mods) n n- Survival and Creative n n- New and old worlds n n n nPlease fix this XP is a vital mechanic and this bug can make worlds feel broken or corrupted. n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Survival world with default settings.
2. Obtain two items with the Mending enchantment, such as a diamond sword and a diamond pickaxe.
3. Equip the diamond sword in your main hand and the diamond pickaxe in your offhand.
4. Engage in combat by attacking mobs, or collect XP orbs from any source (e.g., killing mobs, mining ores).
5. Observe that the experience points do not increase in the player's XP bar.
6. Remove both the diamond sword and diamond pickaxe from your hands.
7. Check the XP bar again; note that it still does not increase unless all items with Mending in your inventory are removed.
8. Confirm that the bug persists regardless of the player's actions or the environment, until all Mending items are completely removed from both hands and the inventory.
Observed Behavior:
When holding two items with the Mending enchantment—one in the main hand (e.g., a sword) and one in the offhand (e.g., a pickaxe or shield)—the experience points (XP) collected from defeating mobs or picking up XP orbs do not increase the player’s XP bar at all. Instead, the XP appears to be "locked" in a repair loop, as the game prioritizes the Mending mechanics for both items simultaneously. 

Specifically:
- If both items are at full durability, they should not receive any XP for repairs, yet the XP collected during gameplay does not go to the player’s XP bar or to either Mending item. 
- The game seems to allocate the XP to both Mending items, but since they are already full, it effectively results in a deadlock where no XP is processed or added to the player’s experience.
- This issue persists even after removing the Mending items from the main hand and offhand; the XP gain remains broken until all Mending items are cleared from both hands and the player’s inventory.

This behavior is specific to scenarios where the item in the main hand has the Mending enchantment. If the Mending item is removed, XP collection resumes normally. The issue can be replicated in both Survival and Creative modes, across new and existing worlds, indicating it is a consistent bug within the game mechanics related to Mending. 

This bug disrupts the core gameplay experience by preventing players from progressing through XP accumulation, which is essential for enchanting and repairing items.
Expected Behavior: When holding two items with the Mending enchantment, one in the main hand and one in the offhand, the expected behavior is that experience orbs collected should first repair the items as intended, but once both items are at full durability, any additional experience should be added to the player's XP bar. The game should not enter a state where XP collection is halted; instead, it should allow the player to continue gaining experience normally. If the main hand item is used to attack or collect XP, it should prioritize repairing that item first until it is at full durability, at which point the experience should transfer to the player’s XP bar. Additionally, removing the Mending items should restore normal XP gain functionality immediately, allowing the player to progress without needing to clear all Mending items from their inventory. This behavior aligns with the mechanics of the Mending enchantment, which is designed to utilize experience for item repair without hindering player progression.

Summary: NBT "ArmorItems" and "HandItems"(maybe there are some other NBT) can t be used normally.
Description: Sorry,I wrong. n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Java Edition Creative world with default settings.
2. Open the chat and enter the command '/give @p minecraft:diamond_sword{Enchantments:[{id:"minecraft:sharpness",lvl:5}]}' to give yourself a diamond sword with an enchantment.
3. Enter the command '/give @p minecraft:diamond_helmet{Enchantments:[{id:"minecraft:protection",lvl:4}]}' to give yourself a diamond helmet with an enchantment.
4. Open your inventory and confirm that the diamond sword and diamond helmet appear with their respective enchantments.
5. Use the command '/data get entity @p' to view the NBT data of your player.
6. Look for the "ArmorItems" and "HandItems" sections in the NBT data output.
7. Attempt to modify the "ArmorItems" by entering the command '/data modify entity @p ArmorItems set value [{"id":"minecraft:diamond_helmet","Count":1,"tag":{"Enchantments":[{"id":"minecraft:protection","lvl":4}]}}]' and observe if it applies correctly.
8. Attempt to modify the "HandItems" by entering the command '/data modify entity @p HandItems set value [{"id":"minecraft:diamond_sword","Count":1,"tag":{"Enchantments":[{"id":"minecraft:sharpness","lvl":5}]}}]' and observe if it applies correctly.
9. Check if the modified items display correctly in your inventory and if the NBT data reflects the changes made.
10. Repeat steps 7-9 with different items to verify if the issue persists across various NBT items.
Observed Behavior:
The issue pertains to the NBT tags "ArmorItems" and "HandItems" within the player.dat file and potentially other related NBT tags. When attempting to utilize or modify these NBT tags, the expected functionality does not occur. 

1. **ArmorItems**: 
   - The "ArmorItems" tag is supposed to store the armor pieces equipped by the player in a list format. However, when trying to access or modify this tag, it fails to reflect the changes in the player's armor status. For instance, if a player attempts to equip a piece of armor using commands that modify this NBT data, the armor does not appear on the player or does not provide the expected protection values.

2. **HandItems**: 
   - Similarly, the "HandItems" tag, which holds the items currently being held by the player (in their main and off-hand), is also malfunctioning. Items that are supposed to be equipped or held do not show up in the player's hand, and commands meant to change the held items do not execute as intended. For example, using commands to replace an item in the hand does not result in the item being visually or functionally present in the player's hand.

3. **Potential Additional Tags**: 
   - There may be other related NBT tags that are affected, such as "HandDropChances" and "ArmorDropChances," which control the likelihood of items being dropped under certain conditions. If these tags are not functioning correctly, it could lead to unexpected behavior when items are dropped or when the player dies.

Overall, the failure of these NBT tags to operate correctly disrupts gameplay by preventing players from equipping armor and holding items as intended, thereby impacting player experience and game mechanics.
Expected Behavior: When accessing the "ArmorItems" and "HandItems" NBT tags in a player's data, the expected behavior is that these tags should accurately represent the items equipped by the player. Specifically, when a player loads their data, the "ArmorItems" tag should contain a list of items currently worn in the armor slots, and the "HandItems" tag should reflect the items held in the player's hands. Each item should be properly formatted according to the NBT structure, including relevant properties such as durability and enchantments. Additionally, players should be able to manipulate these items through in-game mechanics, such as equipping, unequipping, or using items, without any errors or inconsistencies arising from the data. If the tags are functioning correctly, any changes made in-game to the player's equipped items should be accurately saved and reflected in the NBT data upon next loading.

Summary: Failed to load texture for profile
Description: Cant load skin texture. Use vanilla. Skin selected in browser launcher. After start play in singleplayer multiplayer, after some time i got an error in console n n java.util.concurrent.CompletionException: java.io.UncheckedIOException: java.net.SocketException: A connection attempt failed because the connected party did not properly respond after a period of time, or established connection failed because connected host has failed to respond n n n nhttp - protocol not work for downloading, replace it with httpsWhen try to download skin from java application with http - connection time out always, with https downloading process is done n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Open the Minecraft Launcher and log in with your account.
2. Navigate to the "Skins" section of the launcher.
3. Select a skin from the available options or upload a custom skin that is 64x64 pixels in size.
4. Set the selected skin as your active skin.
5. Start a new Singleplayer world or join an existing Multiplayer server.
6. Wait for a few minutes while playing in the world or on the server.
7. Open the console by pressing F3 + T to monitor for any error messages.
8. Observe the console for the error message: "java.util.concurrent.CompletionException: java.io.UncheckedIOException: java.net.SocketException: A connection attempt failed because the connected party did not properly respond after a period of time, or established connection failed because connected host has failed to respond."
9. Attempt to download the skin again using an HTTP connection and note that it fails due to a timeout.
10. Change the download URL to use HTTPS instead of HTTP and verify that the skin downloads successfully.
Observed Behavior:
When attempting to load a player skin in Minecraft Java Edition, the game fails to retrieve the skin texture, resulting in an error message in the console: "Failed to load texture for profile." This issue occurs after selecting a skin through the browser launcher and starting a singleplayer or multiplayer game. After some time, a `java.util.concurrent.CompletionException` is thrown, indicating a failure to establish a connection due to a `java.io.UncheckedIOException` caused by a `java.net.SocketException`. The specific error message states: "A connection attempt failed because the connected party did not properly respond after a period of time, or established connection failed because connected host has failed to respond."

The underlying issue appears to be related to the use of the HTTP protocol for downloading skin textures, which consistently results in connection timeouts. In contrast, when attempting to download the skin using HTTPS, the process completes successfully without errors. This suggests that the game's current implementation for fetching skin textures over HTTP is unreliable, leading to frequent failures in loading player skins. It is recommended that the game replace HTTP connections with HTTPS to ensure successful downloads and improve overall reliability in loading player skins.
Expected Behavior: When launching Minecraft and selecting a skin through the browser launcher, the game should successfully load the chosen skin without any errors or timeouts. The skin should be displayed correctly in both singleplayer and multiplayer modes, reflecting the selected texture as specified in the player's profile. If the game encounters issues with loading the skin due to network protocols, it should automatically attempt to use a secure HTTPS connection instead of HTTP, ensuring that the skin downloads without interruption. The console should not display any errors related to connection timeouts or socket exceptions, and gameplay should continue smoothly without affecting the player's experience or access to their custom skin.

Summary: Dinnerbone doesn t affect happy ghasts
Description: I cant dinnerbone my happy ghast n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Creative world with default settings.
2. Obtain a name tag from the inventory.
3. Name the name tag "Dinnerbone".
4. Summon a Happy Ghast using the command `/summon happy_ghast ~ ~ ~`.
5. Attempt to use the name tag on the summoned Happy Ghast to apply the Dinnerbone effect.
6. Observe the behavior of the Happy Ghast to confirm it does not flip upside down.
Observed Behavior:
When a player names a Happy Ghast using a name tag with "Dinnerbone," the expected behavior of the mob flipping upside down does not occur. Instead, the Happy Ghast remains in its normal orientation, flying right-side up. This issue persists regardless of whether the Happy Ghast is equipped with a Harness or not. 

**Steps to Reproduce:**
1. Obtain a name tag and rename it to "Dinnerbone."
2. Approach a Happy Ghast and use the name tag on it.
3. Observe that the Happy Ghast does not flip upside down as other mobs do when named "Dinnerbone."

**Expected Behavior:**
Upon naming the Happy Ghast "Dinnerbone," it should flip upside down, similar to other mobs in the game, maintaining its original flying behavior while being displayed in an inverted position. 

This bug affects the visual representation of the Happy Ghast, as players expect all mobs to respond consistently to the "Dinnerbone" name tag mechanic.
Expected Behavior: When a player names a Happy Ghast using a name tag with the name "Dinnerbone," the Happy Ghast should flip upside down, similar to how other mobs behave when named with this tag. This behavior is consistent with the mechanics established in Minecraft, where naming any mob "Dinnerbone" or "Grumm" results in the mob rendering upside down without altering its functionality or interactions. The Happy Ghast should maintain its ability to carry players and fly normally while being displayed in this inverted position.

Summary: Interactions can t be interacted
Description: 1. summon interaction n n n2. Right click to this interaction n n n3. data get entity e[type interaction, limit 1, sort nearest] n n n4. There is not a NBT interaction n n n n n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Creative world with default settings.
2. Open the chat and enter the command `/summon interaction ~ ~ ~` to summon an interaction entity at your current location.
3. Position your character close to the interaction entity.
4. Right-click on the interaction entity to attempt to interact with it.
5. Open the chat again and enter the command `/data get entity @e[type=interaction,limit=1,sort=nearest]` to retrieve the NBT data of the nearest interaction entity.
6. Observe that the NBT data does not contain any interaction-related information.
Observed Behavior:
When an interaction entity is summoned using the command `/summon interaction <x> <y> <z>`, it is expected to register player interactions (both left-click and right-click) and store the UUID of the last player to interact with it. However, the following issues occur:

1. **No Interaction Recognition**: Right-clicking the interaction entity does not trigger any expected behavior, such as playing a sound or registering the interaction. The entity does not respond to player interactions at all, effectively making it non-functional.

2. **Data Retrieval Failure**: When attempting to retrieve data from the interaction entity using the command `data get entity e[type=interaction, limit=1, sort=nearest]`, the expected NBT data (specifically the UUID of the last player to interact and the timestamp of the interaction) is not present. This indicates that the entity is not recording any interaction data.

3. **Absence of NBT Tags**: The interaction entity lacks the necessary NBT tags that should normally be present after a player interacts with it. Specifically, the `player` and `timestamp` fields are missing, which are crucial for tracking interactions.

This behavior suggests a bug in the interaction entity's functionality, as it fails to behave as documented and expected within the game mechanics.
Expected Behavior: When an interaction entity is summoned using the command, it should be interactable by players. Specifically, when a player right-clicks on the interaction entity, the UUID of the player who interacted with it should be recorded, along with a timestamp of the interaction. Additionally, executing the command to retrieve the entity's data should return the expected NBT data, including the player's UUID and the timestamp, confirming that the interaction has been properly logged. If the interaction entity has the response property set to true, a punching sound should play upon left-clicking, and the player's arm should swing when right-clicking, indicating that the interaction is functioning as intended.

Summary: vindicators not spawning on a raid
Description: vindicators not spawning on raids for some reason, meaning no totems n nedit: this bug does not exist i just thought wrong n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Survival world with default settings.
2. Ensure that the world difficulty is set to Normal or Hard.
3. Find or create a village where raids can occur.
4. Obtain the Bad Omen effect by defeating a raid captain or pillager patrol.
5. Wait for the raid to trigger in the village.
6. Observe the waves of illagers that spawn during the raid.
7. Specifically look for vindicators during the raid waves, noting their absence.
8. Repeat the process multiple times to confirm the issue persists across different raids.
Observed Behavior:
During a raid, vindicators are expected to spawn as part of the illager mob composition. Specifically, they should appear in waves, starting from the second wave onwards, with their frequency increasing in later waves and at higher difficulties. On Hard difficulty, vindicators can spawn riding ravagers, forming a vindicator ravager jockey. However, in the reported scenario, vindicators do not spawn at all during the raid, which prevents players from acquiring totems of undying, as these items are dropped exclusively by evokers, who also spawn in later waves of the raid.

The absence of vindicators affects the overall challenge and dynamics of the raid, as they play a crucial role in the mob composition. In Java Edition, the expected behavior is that a certain number of vindicators should spawn in each wave, contributing to the raid's difficulty. If this behavior is not occurring, it may indicate a bug in the raid spawning mechanics or an issue with the specific game version being played.

To summarize, the expected behavior is:
- Vindicators should spawn during raids starting from the second wave.
- Their appearance increases in frequency with each subsequent wave and at higher difficulties.
- The absence of vindicators prevents players from obtaining totems of undying dropped by evokers in later waves.

This issue should be investigated to ensure proper spawning mechanics are functioning as intended.
Expected Behavior: When a raid is initiated, vindicators should spawn as part of the raider mobs, appearing more frequently during later waves and at higher difficulties. Specifically, they can spawn in waves 3 and above on Easy difficulty, and from wave 5 onward on Normal and Hard difficulties. Vindicators may also spawn riding ravagers, creating a vindicator ravager jockey, particularly in the later waves. The expected behavior is that players should encounter vindicators as hostile entities during these raids, allowing them the opportunity to obtain totems of undying from evokers, which also spawn during the raid. If the conditions for a raid are met, vindicators should consistently appear according to the game's mechanics, ensuring players can engage with them as intended during raid events.

Summary: Item duplication with item modify command
Description: Steps to reproduce the issue: n n-place a repeating command block with this command: n nitem modify entity a weapon.mainhand {"function":"minecraft:set_damage","damage": -0.001,"add": true} n n-hold an item with more than 1000 max durability in your hand and it s durability should start decreasing n n-open the inventory and hover the cursor on the slot with the item n n-repeatedly press the key to move the item to another slot and bring it back n n n nExpected result: n nthe item does not duplicate n n n nActual result: n neventually the item will duplicate n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Creative world with default settings.
2. Place a repeating command block on the ground.
3. Enter the command `/item modify entity @a weapon.mainhand {"function":"minecraft:set_damage","damage":-0.001,"add":true}` in the command block.
4. Activate the command block using a Redstone signal (e.g., place a Redstone Torch next to it).
5. Hold an item in your main hand that has a maximum durability greater than 1000 (e.g., a diamond pickaxe).
6. Observe that the durability of the item starts to decrease as the command block continuously modifies it.
7. Open your inventory and navigate to the slot containing the item you are holding.
8. Hover your cursor over the item in the inventory slot.
9. Rapidly press the key to move the item to a different slot and then back to its original slot multiple times (at least 10 times).
10. Monitor the item in your inventory to see if it duplicates during this process.
Observed Behavior:
When the player follows the steps outlined to reproduce the bug, the item in the main hand begins to lose durability as expected. However, after repeatedly moving the item to another slot in the inventory and then bringing it back, the item will eventually duplicate within the player's inventory. This duplication occurs despite the expected behavior that the item should not duplicate. 

Specifically, the duplication can be observed as follows:

1. The player holds an item with a durability greater than 1000, such as a diamond tool or armor piece.
2. As the durability decreases from the item modify command, the player can manipulate the item by moving it between inventory slots.
3. After several attempts of moving the item back and forth, the original item remains, and a new instance of the item appears in the player's inventory.

This results in the player having two identical items, which contradicts the expected outcome where the item should remain singular and only lose durability. The issue seems to stem from how the game processes the item modify command in conjunction with inventory management, leading to unintended duplication. The duplication can lead to potential exploits, especially in survival gameplay, where players can gain an unfair advantage by duplicating high-durability items.
Expected Behavior: When using the item modify command with a repeating command block to adjust the durability of an item in the player's main hand, the expected behavior is that the item's durability should decrease as intended without creating a duplicate item. Specifically, when the player follows the steps to modify the item and attempts to move it between inventory slots, the item should remain singular, maintaining its integrity and not allowing for duplication. The game should accurately reflect the item's durability change without generating additional copies, thereby adhering to the intended mechanics of item management in Minecraft.

Summary: Oxidised Copper Golem doesn t turn into Oxidised Copper Golem Statue
Description: Description: Oxidised Copper Golem doesn t turn into Oxidised Copper Golem Statue n n Ways to Reproduce: Create a new creative world, and use the Oxidised Copper Block and Carved Pumpking items to create multiple Copper Golems n nAdditionally you can do tick rate 400 or something like that (note: changing the randomtickspeed doesn t affect Copper Golems and it is already reported as a bug) n n Expected Result: The Oxidised Copper Golems would become Oxidised Copper Golem Statues n n Observed Result: They don t n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Creative world with default settings.
2. In your inventory, locate and select the Oxidized Copper Block and Carved Pumpkin items.
3. Place the Oxidized Copper Block on the ground.
4. Use the Carved Pumpkin on top of the Oxidized Copper Block to create a Copper Golem.
5. Repeat steps 3 and 4 to create multiple Copper Golems in the world.
6. Wait for a period of time to allow the Copper Golems to oxidize naturally.
7. Observe the Copper Golems to see if they transform into Oxidized Copper Golem Statues.
8. Note that changing the random tick speed (e.g., setting it to 400) does not affect the transformation of the Copper Golems into statues.
Observed Behavior:
The Oxidised Copper Golem does not transition into the Oxidised Copper Golem Statue as expected. Upon creating multiple Copper Golems using Oxidised Copper Blocks and Carved Pumpkins in a new creative world, the golems remain in their animated form indefinitely. Despite waiting for a significant duration and even attempting to increase the tick rate to 400 (noting that changing the random tick speed does not influence the behavior of Copper Golems), the transformation does not occur. 

This behavior contradicts the expected result, where the Oxidised Copper Golem should eventually become a stationary Oxidised Copper Golem Statue after a certain period of time or specific conditions being met. The lack of transformation indicates a potential issue with the mechanics governing the lifecycle of the Copper Golem, specifically regarding its oxidation and subsequent conversion into a statue form. 

The inability for the Copper Golem to transition into its statue form may hinder gameplay experiences that rely on the aesthetic and functional aspects of the Oxidised Copper Golem Statue. Further investigation into the triggering conditions for this transformation is necessary to resolve this bug.
Expected Behavior: When multiple Copper Golems are created using Oxidised Copper Blocks and Carved Pumpkins, the expected behavior is that these Copper Golems should gradually oxidize over time and eventually transform into Oxidised Copper Golem Statues. This transformation should occur according to the game's mechanics, where the Copper Golem enters a state of oxidation, leading to its conversion into the statue form. The process should be visually represented with appropriate animations and effects, reflecting the intended functionality as outlined in Minecraft's design for Copper Golems and their interactions with oxidation.

Summary: game behaves as if the reducedDebugInfo gamerule was set to true even when it s false
Description: Most options in the F3 F5 screen are greyed out and say Not visible when debug info is reduced when I hover over the text. n nF3 B and F3 G also don t work regardless of the gamerule. n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Launch Minecraft and create a new world in Creative mode with default settings.
2. Open the chat window and enter the command `/gamerule reducedDebugInfo false` to ensure the gamerule is set to false.
3. Press the F3 key to open the debug screen.
4. Observe that most options in the debug screen (F3 + F5) are still greyed out and display "Not visible" when hovered over.
5. Press F3 + B to check if entity hitboxes are displayed; note that they do not appear.

Summary: mobs in spawners that spawn in water will only spawn in natural water
Description: make a big box and fill it with water source blocks and put a spawner in the box then place a egg in the spawner that would need water to spawn if in the mob spawner n ni expected the mobs to spawn in the water n nsome of the mobs that need water to spawn from mob spawners will only spawn in naturally generated water but will not spawn in artificial water ex: axolotl, squid, tropical fish, cod but others ex: elder guardian, tadpole when placed in a mob spawner and in the water source box spawned in the artificial water n ni tried this in a superflat default world and a creative world in the lukewarm ocean and ice spikes biome n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Creative world with default settings.
2. Build a large box (at least 10x10x5 blocks) using any solid block, ensuring there are no gaps.
3. Fill the entire box with water source blocks, ensuring the water is at the same level throughout the box.
4. Place a monster spawner block inside the water-filled box.
5. Obtain a spawn egg for a water-dependent mob, such as an axolotl, squid, tropical fish, or cod.
6. Right-click the spawner with the spawn egg to set it to spawn the chosen water mob.
7. Wait for the spawner to activate and observe the spawning behavior of the mob.
8. Note that the water mobs do not spawn in the artificial water created in the box, while other mobs like elder guardians or tadpoles spawn correctly if placed in the same spawner. 
9. Repeat steps 1-8 in a Superflat world and in biomes such as the lukewarm ocean and ice spikes to confirm the issue persists across different environments.
Observed Behavior:
When a mob spawner is placed within an artificial water source block environment, certain aquatic mobs that are designed to spawn in water do not spawn as expected. Specifically, mobs such as axolotls, squids, tropical fish, and cod fail to spawn in artificially created water (e.g., water-filled boxes made from water source blocks). In contrast, other aquatic mobs such as elder guardians and tadpoles successfully spawn in the same artificial water setup.

To reproduce this behavior, a player can create a large box filled with water source blocks and place a mob spawner inside. After inserting the corresponding spawn eggs for the aquatic mobs that require water to spawn, it is observed that the aforementioned mobs do not appear. This issue persists in various game environments, including a superflat default world and creative mode in biomes such as the lukewarm ocean and ice spikes biome.

Key observations include:
- Aquatic mobs that require water to spawn (e.g., axolotls, squids, tropical fish, cod) only spawn in naturally generated water bodies.
- Other aquatic mobs (e.g., elder guardians, tadpoles) can spawn in artificial water created by the player.
- This behavior has been tested across different worlds and biomes, confirming the inconsistency in spawning mechanics for different aquatic mobs. 

The discrepancy in spawning conditions between natural and artificial water sources for these aquatic mobs needs to be addressed to ensure consistent gameplay mechanics.
Expected Behavior: When a mob spawner is placed in an artificial body of water created by the player, it should spawn aquatic mobs that require water, such as axolotls, squids, tropical fish, and cod, regardless of whether the water is natural or artificially created. The expected behavior is that all aquatic mobs compatible with the spawner should appear in the water, as long as the spawning conditions are met, similar to how other mobs spawn in their respective environments. This includes complying with the necessary water block requirements for each specific mob type. Additionally, mobs like elder guardians and tadpoles should also spawn in the artificial water if they are placed in the spawner, reflecting the intended functionality of mob spawners in Minecraft.

Summary: status effects duration continues to decrement while on pause screen in single player
Description: To reproduce, create a world, give yourself a status effect of a set duration, and pause the game for that duration. Once the duration has elapsed, unpause the game and check if the status effect is still applied. n nIt would be expected that the timer is paused when the game is paused. For example, drinking a potion and pausing the game should not consume the duration of that potion. n nCurrently, status effects duration continue to decrement while on the pause screen in single player. n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Single Player world in Creative mode with default settings.
2. Open the inventory and locate a potion that grants a status effect, such as a Potion of Swiftness.
3. Drink the potion to apply the status effect, noting the duration displayed in seconds.
4. Immediately pause the game by pressing the 'Esc' key.
5. Wait for the duration of the status effect to elapse while the game is paused.
6. After the duration has elapsed, unpause the game by pressing the 'Esc' key again.
7. Check the status effect icon in the effects menu to see if the effect is still active and for how long it remains.
Observed Behavior:
When a player applies a status effect with a defined duration (e.g., by consuming a potion or being affected by a mob), the countdown for that effect continues to decrease even when the game is paused. The expected behavior is that the timer for the status effect should halt while the game is in the pause state, meaning that the duration should not elapse during this time.

To demonstrate this issue, a player can create a single-player world, apply a status effect (such as Speed or Strength) with a specific duration, and then pause the game immediately after. Upon resuming the game after the duration of the effect has passed, the player will find that the status effect is no longer active, despite having been paused for the entire duration. 

For example, if a player drinks a Potion of Swiftness, which grants Speed for 3 minutes (360 seconds), and then pauses the game for the entire duration of that effect, they should still have the Speed effect upon unpausing. However, the current behavior results in the effect being lost because the timer continues to decrement while the game is paused.

This issue can lead to confusion among players, as it contradicts the intuitive understanding that pausing the game should also pause all ongoing effects and timers.
Expected Behavior: When a player is on the pause screen in single player mode, the duration of any active status effects should not decrease. Specifically, if a player consumes a potion that grants a status effect with a defined duration, the timer for that effect should pause while the game is paused. Upon resuming gameplay, the remaining duration of the status effect should reflect the time that was left when the game was paused, ensuring that the player experiences the full intended effect duration. This behavior aligns with the expected mechanics of status effects in Minecraft, where effects are designed to be active only during gameplay and should not diminish while the game is not actively running.

Summary: Copper Golem memory does not register checked blocks in a proper order
Description: 1. Setup a line with 10 chests with any item n n n2. Take notice of the coordinates of each chest n n n3. Place a copper chest with an item that is not contained in any other chest n n n4. Take notice of the order in which the golem open the chests n n n5. Wait for the golem to open the last chest, then immediatly use tick freeze n n n6. Use data get command to access the checked blocks list in the golem s memory n n n7. Compare the coordinates of the blocks in the list and the order observed in step 4 n n n n n nExpected Result: the order of the list in the golem s memory would be the same as the order in which it opend the chests. n nActual Result: the order os the list in the golem memories does not correspond to the order in which the chests were opend. n n n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Creative world with default settings.
2. Place 10 standard chests in a straight line, ensuring they are adjacent to one another.
3. Fill each chest with a different item, noting the coordinates of each chest (e.g., Chest 1 at x, y, z).
4. Place a copper golem nearby.
5. Place a copper chest with an item that is not contained in any of the 10 chests.
6. Observe the order in which the copper golem opens the chests, taking note of the sequence.
7. Wait for the copper golem to open the last chest in the sequence.
8. Immediately after the golem opens the last chest, use the command '/gamerule randomTickSpeed 0' to freeze the game ticks.
9. Use the command '/data get entity @e[type=minecraft:copper_golem,limit=1] Memory' to access the checked blocks list in the golem's memory.
10. Compare the coordinates in the checked blocks list with the order observed in step 6 to check for discrepancies.
Observed Behavior:
Upon executing the steps outlined in the bug report, the Copper Golem exhibits unexpected behavior regarding its memory of the checked blocks. Specifically, after the Copper Golem has opened all 10 chests in the setup, the order of the chest openings as observed does not match the order of the coordinates stored in the golem's memory.

1. **Observation of Opening Order**: The player notes the sequence in which the Copper Golem opens the chests, which is expected to be a linear progression from the first to the last chest based on their arrangement.

2. **Memory Retrieval**: After freezing the game tick and accessing the golem's memory using the `/data get` command, the player retrieves the list of checked blocks.

3. **Comparison of Orders**: Upon comparing the order of the coordinates from the golem's memory with the observed order from the chest openings, discrepancies are found. The coordinates in the memory do not reflect the same sequence as the order in which the chests were opened by the golem.

4. **Specific Findings**: The actual order of coordinates in the golem's memory may appear randomized or grouped in a manner that does not correspond to the logical progression of the chest openings. This indicates a potential issue in how the Copper Golem's memory registers and stores the sequence of checked blocks, leading to confusion in behavior expectations.

This inconsistency suggests a bug in the Copper Golem's algorithm for processing and remembering the order of interacted blocks, which could affect its functionality in gameplay scenarios where precise memory of interactions is crucial.
Expected Behavior: When the Copper Golem interacts with a series of chests, it should sequentially open each chest in the order they were placed, starting from the first to the last. The expected behavior is that the order of chests opened will be accurately reflected in the Copper Golem's memory as a list of checked blocks. Specifically, when using the data get command to access the Golem's memory, the coordinates of the checked blocks should match the order in which the chests were opened, confirming that the Golem registers interactions in a linear and predictable manner. This functionality aligns with Minecraft's mechanics for entity interactions, ensuring that entities maintain a consistent and logical record of their actions.

Summary: Sprint Window slider shows useless values
Description: You cannot sprint by double-tapping W after setting the sprint window option to 1 or 2. It seems that this duration includes both movement start ticks (see attachment), which means that setting the option to 2 would require you to press W once in two consecutive ticks (which just gets interpreted as holding W), and setting it to 1 requires two presses within a single tick (impossible due to how the game registers inputs). n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Minecraft world in Creative mode with default settings.
2. Open the game settings and navigate to the "Controls" section.
3. Locate the "Sprint Window" option and set it to "1" or "2".
4. Exit the settings menu to return to the game.
5. Press the "W" key to start moving forward.
6. Attempt to double-tap the "W" key to initiate sprinting.
7. Observe that the character does not sprint and remains at walking speed.
8. Repeat steps 5-7 while varying the timing of the double-tap to confirm that sprinting is not activated.
Observed Behavior:
When the sprint window option is set to 1 or 2, the player is unable to sprint by double-tapping the forward key (W). The sprint window setting determines the number of game ticks that must pass between each double-tap for the sprint to activate. However, the current implementation includes both the initial movement start ticks and the double-tap requirement, leading to unintended behavior:

1. **Sprint Window Setting of 2:** 
   - The player must press W once during two consecutive ticks. This effectively translates to the game interpreting the input as holding the W key rather than recognizing it as a double-tap, thus preventing sprinting.

2. **Sprint Window Setting of 1:** 
   - The player is required to press W twice within a single tick. Given the input registration mechanics of the game, this is impossible to achieve, as the game cannot register two distinct key presses within the same tick.

This results in the sprint window settings of 1 and 2 being effectively useless, as neither allows the player to initiate sprinting through the intended double-tap mechanic. The expected behavior of activating sprinting through double-tapping W is not achievable under these settings, leading to confusion and frustration for players who adjust their sprint settings in hopes of improving their gameplay experience.
Expected Behavior: When the sprint window option is set to 1 or 2, the player should be able to initiate sprinting by double-tapping the forward movement key (W) as intended. Specifically, if the sprint window is set to 2, the player should be able to double-tap W within two consecutive ticks to start sprinting, and if it is set to 1, the player should be able to double-tap W within a single tick. This behavior aligns with the official mechanics of sprinting in Minecraft, where players can sprint by either double-tapping the forward key or holding the sprint key while moving. The game should accurately register these inputs without requiring impossible timing, allowing players to effectively utilize the sprinting mechanic as documented in the game guides.

Summary: when an invisible item frame is given to the player using commands, then the game gets updated to a newer version, the game crashes.
Description: i made a invisible item frame using commands. it was in my inventory and when placed, seemed invisible. The item frame worked like a normal item frame. for some reason though, when i updated to the new snapshot (25w31a) clicking the item frame to put in my inventory crashed my game. i tried using a hotbar indicator, which worked. I could not fix the chest problem though.(this works in shulkers as well. n n n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Creative world with default settings.
2. Open the chat and enter the command to give yourself an invisible item frame: `/give @p minecraft:item_frame{Invisible:1}`.
3. Verify that the invisible item frame is now in your inventory.
4. Place the invisible item frame in the world by selecting it from your inventory and right-clicking on the ground.
5. Confirm that the item frame appears invisible but functions normally by placing an item inside it.
6. Save and exit the world.
7. Update the game to the newer snapshot version (25w31a).
8. Re-enter the world containing the invisible item frame.
9. Attempt to click on the invisible item frame to pick it up and put it back into your inventory.
10. Observe that the game crashes upon attempting to pick up the invisible item frame.
Observed Behavior:
When an invisible item frame is created using commands and placed in the inventory, it functions as a normal item frame but remains visually invisible. Upon updating the game to the snapshot version 25w31a, attempting to interact with this invisible item frame by clicking on it to retrieve it from the inventory results in a game crash. 

Additional testing revealed that the issue is not isolated to item frames; it also occurs with shulker boxes containing items. While using a hotbar indicator allows players to interact with the item frame without crashing, there is no known workaround for the crash when attempting to retrieve the item frame directly from the inventory. This behavior has been consistently reproducible and suggests a potential bug in the game's handling of invisible item frames after version updates. 

**Steps to Reproduce:**
1. Use commands to create an invisible item frame.
2. Place the invisible item frame in the player's inventory.
3. Update the game to snapshot version 25w31a.
4. Click on the invisible item frame in the inventory to attempt to retrieve it.

**Expected Behavior:**
The player should be able to retrieve the invisible item frame from their inventory without causing the game to crash. 

**Additional Context:**
- The crash occurs specifically when clicking on the invisible item frame in the inventory.
- The issue also manifests with shulker boxes that have been similarly created or manipulated.
- Players can still interact with the item frame using hotbar indicators without crashing, indicating that the issue is related to direct interactions with the inventory.
Expected Behavior: When a player is given an invisible item frame using commands, the expected behavior is that the item frame should function normally without causing any crashes, regardless of the game version. The player should be able to place the invisible item frame in the world, interact with it, and store items within it without issues. Upon updating to a newer version, such as snapshot 25w31a, the game should retain compatibility with the invisible item frame, allowing the player to retrieve it from their inventory without experiencing a crash. The mechanics of item frames, including their visibility and functionality, should operate as documented, ensuring a stable gaming experience.

Summary: A bug where hostile mobs don t spawn in Realms.
Description: Hostile mobs do not spawn, or spawn very rarely, even at night. Changing the game difficulty to reset mob spawning doesn t help. The issue doesn t occur before the chunk is loaded, but once the chunk has been loaded, hostile mobs stop spawning completely. n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Minecraft Realm with default settings and ensure the game mode is set to Survival.
2. Wait until nighttime in the Realm, ensuring that it is fully dark outside.
3. Observe the area around you, specifically looking for any hostile mobs such as zombies, skeletons, or creepers spawning in the vicinity.
4. Change the game difficulty to Easy, then back to Normal, and observe if this affects the spawning of hostile mobs.
5. Move to a location where you know hostile mobs typically spawn, such as a plains biome or a dark cave.
6. Remain in the area for an extended period (at least 10 minutes) without leaving the chunk to see if any hostile mobs spawn.
7. If no hostile mobs appear, exit the chunk (by moving more than 128 blocks away) and then return to the same location to check for spawning again.
8. Repeat the process of waiting at least 10 minutes in the loaded chunk to confirm that hostile mobs do not spawn after the initial loading.
Observed Behavior:
In the current state of the bug, hostile mobs are failing to spawn within loaded chunks on Minecraft Realms, despite the presence of appropriate spawning conditions. Specifically, the following behaviors have been observed:

1. **Time of Day:** Hostile mobs, which typically spawn at night or in low light levels, do not appear even during nighttime. This is contrary to expected behavior where mobs like zombies, skeletons, and creepers should spawn in dark areas.

2. **Chunk Loading:** The issue manifests once a chunk has been loaded. Initially, hostile mobs may spawn correctly when the chunk is first generated, but upon subsequent loading, they cease to spawn entirely. This indicates that the problem may be linked to how the game handles mob spawning after the initial chunk generation.

3. **Game Difficulty Settings:** Changing the game difficulty (e.g., from Easy to Hard) does not resolve the issue. In normal circumstances, modifying difficulty is expected to reset the mob spawning mechanics, but in this case, it has no effect on the absence of hostile mobs.

4. **Mob Cap Considerations:** The spawning issue does not appear to be related to the mob cap, as there are no persistent hostile mobs present in the vicinity that would prevent new ones from spawning. The expected cap for hostile mobs is 70, which should not be reached under normal gameplay.

5. **Spawn Conditions:** Hostile mobs should spawn in areas with a light level of 7 or lower. The absence of hostile mobs suggests that the game is not recognizing or processing the necessary conditions for spawning, even when they are met.

6. **Testing Environment:** This bug has been consistently reproduced in various Realms settings, indicating it is not an isolated incident. Players across different platforms (Java and Bedrock) have reported similar issues, suggesting a broader problem with the Realms environment.

In summary, the bug presents a significant disruption to gameplay, as players are unable to encounter or engage with hostile mobs, which are essential for survival and combat mechanics in Minecraft. The issue requires investigation to determine its root cause and to restore expected mob spawning behavior in Realms.
Expected Behavior: When a player is in a loaded chunk at night and the difficulty is set to any level other than Peaceful, hostile mobs should spawn regularly according to the game's mechanics. This includes common hostile mobs like zombies, skeletons, and creepers appearing in dark areas or at night, as long as the light level is below 8. Additionally, the spawning should not be hindered by any environmental factors such as excessive light sources or blocks that prevent mob spawning. If conditions are met, hostile mobs should spawn consistently within a 128-block radius of the player without any significant delays or interruptions. The expected behavior aligns with Minecraft's design, where hostile mobs are intended to actively spawn in appropriate conditions, contributing to the game's survival challenges.

Upon unpausing the game after an extended period of inactivity, the frame rate drops significantly, fluctuating between 5 and 30 FPS. This performance issue persists and does not stabilize back to normal levels, which typically range from 60 FPS or higher under standard gameplay conditions. The FPS drop occurs regardless of the world settings or the number of entities present in the game. 

This issue has been observed multiple times in the same snapshot and seems to occur consistently after the game has been paused for a prolonged duration (generally more than 10 minutes). During this period, the game appears to maintain its resource allocation, but upon resuming, it struggles to render frames efficiently, leading to a choppy and unplayable experience. 

Players can verify the FPS drop by accessing the debug screen using the F3 key, which displays the current frame rate alongside other performance metrics. This fluctuation does not seem to correlate with any specific in-game events, such as chunk loading or high entity counts, as it occurs independently of these factors. 

Additionally, restarting the game or reloading the world does not resolve the issue, indicating that it may be related to the game's handling of resources after being paused. This behavior has been reported by multiple users, suggesting it may be a broader issue affecting players in this snapshot.
Expected Behavior: When a player unpauses Minecraft after being in the pause menu, the game should resume at a stable frame rate, ideally returning to the previously experienced FPS levels before the pause. The game should not exhibit any significant fluctuations in frame rate, and performance should remain consistent with the expected behavior of the game. If the player's system is capable of running Minecraft at a higher FPS, it should maintain that performance without dropping to lower levels, ensuring a smooth and seamless gaming experience. The game should also properly manage resources and not suffer from performance degradation after being paused for an extended period, as outlined in the official guidelines for optimizing frame rates in Minecraft.

Summary: There is a bug where 4 moon phases are over the sun and 4 moons below the sun
Description: This has happened since some 1.21.6 snapshots and the official 1.21.6 update n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Creative world with default settings.
2. Set the game time to night by entering the command '/time set night'.
3. Use the command '/time set 14000' to set the moon to the full moon phase.
4. Observe the sky and note the position of the moon relative to the sun.
5. Use the command '/time add 24000' to advance the time by one full day.
6. Observe the sky again to confirm that there are now 4 moon phases appearing above the sun and 4 moons appearing below the sun.
7. Repeat steps 5 and 6 multiple times to verify that the issue persists across multiple day cycles.
Observed Behavior:
In the current version of Minecraft, specifically since some of the 1.21.6 snapshots and the official 1.21.6 update, an unusual rendering bug occurs where multiple moon phases are visible simultaneously. This results in a visual anomaly where four distinct moon phases are displayed above the sun and four additional phases are rendered below it. 

1. **Moon Phases Displayed**: Each of the eight lunar phases—New Moon, Waxing Crescent, First Quarter, Waxing Gibbous, Full Moon, Waning Gibbous, Last Quarter, and Waning Crescent—appears incorrectly in the sky. This creates a cluttered visual effect that does not align with the expected behavior of the moon being opposite the sun.

2. **Visibility Conditions**: The bug manifests regardless of the time of day or weather conditions, consistently showing all eight phases at once in the sky.

3. **Game Mechanics Impact**: While this bug does not appear to affect gameplay mechanics directly, it disrupts the visual aesthetics of the game and could potentially confuse players regarding the current moon phase, which is relevant for certain gameplay elements such as mob spawning.

4. **Reproducibility**: The issue has been reproducible across multiple worlds and does not seem to be tied to any specific world settings or configurations.

This bug has persisted through various snapshots leading up to the official release of version 1.21.6, indicating a possible rendering or graphical issue introduced in this update.
Expected Behavior: The expected behavior is that the moon should consistently be positioned opposite the sun in the sky, following the established mechanics of the game's celestial bodies. This means that at any given time, if the sun is visible in the sky, the moon should not be present, and vice versa. The moon should also progress through its eight distinct phases in a predictable manner, transitioning smoothly at the end of each in-game day. Therefore, it is not possible for multiple moon phases to appear simultaneously above and below the sun, as this contradicts the fundamental design of the game's celestial mechanics.

Summary: Some entities freeze upon entering this chunk
Description: When some entities touch this chunk, they freeze instantly and stop receiving damage from effects like fire. Despite this, these entities can be damaged by the player, but just one first time; after that, they stop taking damage. n nProjectiles and thrown items are affected too. Tamed horses don t freeze just if you are on them, but you can t dismount them while being in the chunk. n nIf a mob is killed by explosions from another chunk, the body remains on the floor, not ending the death animation, but turning the mob red and throwing the mob drop. This was tested with a creeper explosion. n nSpecific cases are, for example: n n A zombie burning down doesn t die, and it doesn t start burning if it entered at night. n n n A witch keeps the potion effect that was taking when entering the chunk. n n n Endermen can t teleport; in those cases, the particles are still moving. n n n Arrows don t do damage to the player. n n n A tamed horse stops freezing if it s mounted. n n n Items can t be taken and start popping falling infinitely. n n n Shulkers can t move or open, but their projectiles still move in a straight line and despair at a range. n n n TNT makes the explosion animation but just disappears without making any damage. n n n nWe were playing on a server. The seed where we found it was -9101802231045030160 , at XYZ: 166 100 -270 . When all players exit the world, the bug disappears, making all effects play together. n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Multiplayer server using the seed -9101802231045030160.
2. Teleport to the coordinates XYZ: 166, 100, -270 to reach the specific chunk where the bug occurs.
3. Enter the chunk from an adjacent chunk with any entity (e.g., a player, zombie, or witch) and observe that the entity freezes immediately upon entry.
4. Attempt to set the frozen entity on fire and note that it does not take damage from the fire.
5. Try to damage the frozen entity with a melee attack and confirm that it can be damaged only once; subsequent attacks do not register damage.
6. Shoot arrows at the frozen entity and verify that they do not inflict damage.
7. Throw items into the chunk and observe that they begin to pop and fall infinitely without being collectible.
8. Summon a tamed horse and enter the chunk while mounted to see that the horse does not freeze but cannot be dismounted.
9. Use a Creeper to create an explosion in an adjacent chunk and observe the mob's body remaining on the ground without completing the death animation.
10. Test the behavior of other entities like a witch, enderman, and shulkers by having them enter the chunk and noting their respective effects (e.g., witches retaining potion effects, endermen being unable to teleport, and shulkers being unable to move or open).
11. Exit the server with all players to confirm that the bug disappears and all effects return to normal when the chunk is no longer loaded.
Observed Behavior:
Upon entering the specified chunk, certain entities experience a freezing effect, which causes them to become motionless and stop receiving damage from environmental effects such as fire. This freezing effect is observed in various entity types, including mobs and projectiles. Notably, while players can inflict damage on these frozen entities once, subsequent attempts to deal damage will not register.

The following behaviors have been documented:

1. **Mob Behavior:**
   - **Zombies:** A zombie that enters the chunk while on fire does not die or continue burning, regardless of time of day.
   - **Witches:** Witches retain any active potion effects they had prior to entering the chunk.
   - **Endermen:** Endermen are unable to teleport when they enter the chunk, although their teleportation particles continue to animate.
   - **Shulkers:** Shulkers become immobile and cannot open, but they can still shoot projectiles that travel in a straight line and disappear after a certain range.
   - **TNT:** TNT appears to detonate visually but does not cause any damage or destruction to the surrounding blocks.

2. **Player and Projectile Interaction:**
   - **Arrows:** Arrows shot at players within the chunk do not inflict any damage.
   - **Items:** Items that fall into the chunk will not be collectible and will continue to drop indefinitely, creating a situation where they appear to "pop" endlessly.

3. **Tamed Horses:** 
   - Tamed horses do not freeze if the player is mounted on them. However, players are unable to dismount while in the chunk.

4. **Explosion Behavior:**
   - If a mob is killed by an explosion occurring in an adjacent chunk, the mob's body remains visible and continues the death animation indefinitely, turning red and dropping its items.

This bug was observed on a multiplayer server using the seed -9101802231045030160 at coordinates XYZ: 166, 100, -270. Importantly, when all players exit the world, the freezing effect disappears, allowing all entities to function normally.
Expected Behavior: When entities enter the affected chunk, they should continue to function normally without freezing or becoming unresponsive. For instance, mobs should take damage from fire and other effects as intended, and players should be able to interact with and damage entities without restrictions. Projectiles and thrown items should behave as expected, dealing damage upon impact. Tamed horses should allow players to dismount freely, regardless of the chunk they are in. If a mob is killed by an explosion, it should complete its death animation and drop items as normal, without remaining in a frozen state. Additionally, entities should not retain any effects or states that would prevent them from functioning properly, such as a witch maintaining a potion effect upon entering the chunk or endermen being unable to teleport. All items should be collectible and should not infinitely pop out of existence. TNT should explode and cause damage to nearby blocks as designed, ensuring that all game mechanics operate consistently within the chunk.

Summary: Ender Dragon bring about unusual acceleration
Description: When the Ender Dragon is lingering over the return portal, it has no hitbox except for its head. This allows the player to be positioned inside the dragon s center. At the moment when the Ender Dragon tries to take off again, its hitbox reappears, and the player gets launched away. If the player shoots an arrow at that precise moment, the resulting arrow velocity becomes extremely high not because the actual knockback on the Ender Dragon is particularly strong, but possibly because knockback is still being calculated internally (server-side?). Since arrow velocity is apparently calculated based on the internal values, the closer the player is to the dragon s center, the more abnormal the resulting arrow speed becomes. This is what is being claimed. n nExpected result: Arrow velocity should correspond to the actual knockback experienced by the player.Actual result: Either the internal knockback calculation on the Ender Dragon is absurdly exaggerated, or arrow velocity is being calculated based on internal values at least one of these must be true, resulting in extremely high arrow speeds. n nWhile it s questionable whether knockback behavior should follow a purely exponential function especially considering the changing hitbox it remains unclear how much of this behavior is intentional. n nI don t have the equipment or specs to record a video myself, so I can t demonstrate the behavior in the latest version. However, there is an older version video that explains a bug caused by the same underlying factors, which I believe serves as a helpful reference. It should be reproducible using the same steps. n n
Steps to Reproduce:
1. Create a new Creative world in Minecraft and switch to Survival mode.
2. Locate the End dimension by entering an End Portal or using the command '/execute in the_end run tp @p 0 100 0'.
3. Wait for the Ender Dragon to spawn, which occurs 20 game ticks (1 second) after entering the End.
4. Position yourself directly above the End Portal at coordinates (0, 64, 0) while ensuring the Ender Dragon is hovering above the portal.
5. Move into the center of the Ender Dragon's body, ensuring you are within its hitbox, which is only active for the head at this point.
6. Wait for the Ender Dragon to attempt to take off again, watching for the moment its hitbox reappears.
7. At the exact moment the hitbox reappears, shoot an arrow at the Ender Dragon.
8. Observe the resulting arrow velocity and note any abnormal speeds compared to typical arrow velocities when hitting other entities.
Observed Behavior:
When the Ender Dragon is positioned above the return portal, it exhibits a peculiar interaction with player projectiles and hitbox mechanics. In this state, the dragon's hitbox is only active at its head, allowing players to enter its center without collision. Upon the dragon's attempt to take off, its full hitbox reactivates, resulting in the player being forcefully ejected from the center. 

If the player releases an arrow at the exact moment the dragon's hitbox reappears, the arrow's velocity is significantly amplified. This phenomenon does not stem from an increase in the actual knockback applied to the dragon, but rather indicates that the internal knockback calculations may still be in effect on the server side. The mechanics behind arrow velocity appear to be influenced by the proximity of the player to the dragon's center; as the player gets closer, the resulting arrow speed becomes disproportionately high.

This behavior leads to two potential conclusions: either the internal knockback calculations for the Ender Dragon are excessively exaggerated, or the arrow velocity is calculated based on these internal values rather than the actual knockback experienced by the player. As a result, the arrow speeds can reach levels that are not consistent with expected gameplay mechanics.

In summary, the expected behavior is that arrow velocity should reflect the actual knockback experienced by the player when interacting with the Ender Dragon. However, the observed behavior suggests a discrepancy in the calculations, leading to unnaturally high arrow speeds when the player is positioned at the dragon's center during its takeoff. This interaction raises questions about the intended design of knockback mechanics, particularly in relation to the changing hitbox of the Ender Dragon.
Expected Behavior: When the Ender Dragon is positioned over the return portal, it should maintain a consistent hitbox for the entirety of its hovering behavior, allowing players to interact with it as intended. When the dragon takes off, the hitbox should reappear in a manner that does not cause unexpected player displacement. If a player shoots an arrow at the moment the dragon takes off, the arrow's velocity should be calculated based on the actual knockback experienced by the player from the dragon's movement, rather than any exaggerated internal values. The arrow should not exceed the standard projectile speed limits set by the game's mechanics, ensuring that its behavior aligns with expected physics and gameplay dynamics. This would preserve the integrity of combat interactions with the Ender Dragon, allowing for a fair and consistent player experience.

:
